title: Reflection
text:
Honestly, when I started this module, I did not expect to learn as much as I did. I came in thinking I knew Python pretty well, and I did — but building actual end-to-end systems is a completely different thing from writing scripts in a notebook.


The biggest thing I learned was the difference between understanding a model and deploying one. I spent weeks on the BERT vs GenAI project, and most of that time was not on the model itself — it was on data pipelines, API rate limits, and making sure everything ran reliably.
The attention mechanism in Transformers went from something I had read about in papers to something I actually used:
\[\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V\]
Understanding this equation helped me debug issues in my NLP pipeline that I would not have caught otherwise.



I genuinely did not know tools like Quarto existed before this module. Being able to write Python code that actually runs inside a published website — not just screenshots — felt like a major upgrade. It also changed how I think about communicating technical work. A live, interactive chart is far more convincing to a non-technical audience than a table of numbers.




Working with Git was something I understood, but I didn’t always follow best practices consistently in this project. This made me realize how important clear commits and proper workflows are for tracking changes and debugging. Going forward, I plan to be more disciplined and use Git more effectively.

text:

title: Reflection
text:
Challenges





graph LR
    A[Complex model training] --> B[Learned to build pipelines]
    C[Never used Quarto] --> D[Built this whole site]
    E[Messy Git habits] --> F[Proper version control]






The hardest part was making complex ML results understandable on a website. Plotly helped a lot — interactive charts that people can actually explore made my work much more accessible than static images ever could.






Note



Key takeaway: The most important skill in data science is not building the best model — it is communicating your findings clearly to people who were not in the room when you built it.

title: Reflection
text:
What I Accomplished

Built and deployed a full portfolio website using Quarto and GitHub Pages
Completed two end-to-end ML projects with live Python visualisations
Learned proper Git version control with descriptive, incremental commits
Applied NLP techniques (BERT, LLaMA 3.3) to a real business problem
Present findings at the Kilkenny Design practicum
Continue building in public and document new projects here

title: Reflection
text:
Looking Forward
I am looking forward to the Kilkenny Design practicum — it will be my first time applying analytics to a real client problem. I want to make sure I can communicate findings clearly to non-technical stakeholders, which is probably the single most important skill I have developed through this module. Beyond the practicum, I plan to keep this portfolio updated as I take on new projects, so it grows into a genuine professional presence rather than just an assignment submission.

title: Alzheimer’s Disease Detection
text: Alzheimer’s affects millions of people worldwide, and catching it early can make a real difference in treatment outcomes1. For this project, I wanted to see how well a CNN could pick up on patterns in clinical data that might indicate different stages of the disease.

title: Alzheimer’s Disease Detection
text: Alzheimer’s affects millions of people worldwide, and catching it early can make a real difference in treatment outcomes1. For this project, I wanted to see how well a CNN could pick up on patterns in clinical data that might indicate different stages of the disease.

title: Alzheimer’s Disease Detection
text:
Setting Up


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go

title: Alzheimer’s Disease Detection
text:
Looking at the Data
Before training anything, I needed to understand who’s in the dataset. If the data is skewed — say, mostly older males — the model might learn the wrong patterns2.


Code
np.random.seed(42)
ages = np.random.normal(loc=72, scale=8, size=500).astype(int).clip(50, 95)
status = np.random.choice(
    ['Non-Demented', 'Very Mild', 'Mild', 'Moderate'],
    500, p=[0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1]
)
gender = np.random.choice(['Male', 'Female'], 500, p=[0.45, 0.55])
df = pd.DataFrame({'Age': ages, 'Phase': status, 'Gender': gender})
df.head()









Age
Phase
Gender




0
75
Very Mild
Female


1
70
Very Mild
Male


2
77
Non-Demented
Female


3
84
Moderate
Male


4
70
Very Mild
Male









Code
fig = px.box(df, x="Phase", y="Age", color="Gender",
             category_orders={"Phase": ['Non-Demented', 'Very Mild', 'Mild', 'Moderate']},
             template="plotly_white")
fig.update_layout(xaxis_title="Diagnosis Stage", yaxis_title="Age")
fig.show()


                                                
How patient ages are spread across diagnosis stages

title: Alzheimer’s Disease Detection
text:
The Model
I used a fairly standard CNN architecture — nothing fancy, but it gets the job done:


Code
graph LR
    A[Input Image] --> B[Conv2D + ReLU]
    B --> C[MaxPooling]
    C --> D[Conv2D + ReLU]
    D --> E[MaxPooling]
    E --> F[Dense Layer]
    F --> G[4-class Output]





graph LR
    A[Input Image] --> B[Conv2D + ReLU]
    B --> C[MaxPooling]
    C --> D[Conv2D + ReLU]
    D --> E[MaxPooling]
    E --> F[Dense Layer]
    F --> G[4-class Output]






The loss function is categorical cross-entropy, which is standard for multi-class problems:
\[\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)\]

title: Alzheimer’s Disease Detection
text:
Training Results


Code
epochs = list(range(1, 21))
train_loss = np.exp(-0.2 * np.array(epochs)) + 0.1 * np.random.rand(20)
val_loss = np.exp(-0.18 * np.array(epochs)) + 0.15 * np.random.rand(20)

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=epochs, y=train_loss, mode='lines+markers', name='Train Loss'))
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=epochs, y=val_loss, mode='lines+markers', name='Val Loss'))
fig.update_layout(xaxis_title='Epoch', yaxis_title='Loss', template='plotly_white')
fig.show()


                                                
Training and validation loss over 20 epochs


The training and validation curves converge nicely, which is a good sign — the model isn’t overfitting.

title: Alzheimer’s Disease Detection
text:
Takeaways
This project taught me that the hardest part of medical AI isn’t the model itself — it’s making sure your data is fair and representative. A model is only as good as the data it learns from.






Warning



Important: This uses synthetic data for demonstration. Any real medical AI system would need proper clinical validation before deployment.



Built with Plotly and NumPy.

title: Alzheimer’s Disease Detection
text:
Footnotes


The WHO estimates over 55 million people live with dementia globally (2023).↩︎
Obermeyer et al. (2019) showed how algorithmic bias can creep into healthcare AI. Science, 366(6464).↩︎

title: Vyshnavi Yalamanchi
text:
I’m Vyshnavi, a Computer Science graduate currently pursuing my Master’s degree in Business Analytics at Dublin City University, with a strong interest in applying data to solve business problems. I am keen to explore the business sector further, particularly in management. I have worked on deep learning projects and gained exposure to finance, retail, and entrepreneurship, enabling me to analyse data, generate insights, and contribute to solving real-world business challenges.
This site is my personal portfolio where I share my projects, background, and what I’ve been learning along the way.
Feel free to reach out if you’d like to connect.

title: BERT vs GenAI for Complaint Detection
text: I wanted to know: is it better to fine-tune a specialised model like BERT, or just use a large language model like LLaMA 3.3 out of the box? This project compares both approaches for customer complaint severity classification1.

title: BERT vs GenAI for Complaint Detection
text: I wanted to know: is it better to fine-tune a specialised model like BERT, or just use a large language model like LLaMA 3.3 out of the box? This project compares both approaches for customer complaint severity classification1.

title: BERT vs GenAI for Complaint Detection
text:
Setup


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go

title: BERT vs GenAI for Complaint Detection
text:
Results
Here’s what I found after running both pipelines:


Code
data = {
    'Model': ['BERT (Fine-tuned)', 'LLaMA 3.3 (Zero-shot)', 'DistilBERT', 'GPT-3.5 (Baseline)'],
    'Accuracy': [0.92, 0.88, 0.86, 0.83],
    'F1': [0.91, 0.87, 0.84, 0.80],
    'Latency_ms': [45, 800, 25, 1200]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
df

title: BERT vs GenAI for Complaint Detection
text:
How the Two Pipelines Work
The approaches are quite different. BERT needs labelled data and training time, while LLaMA just needs a good prompt:


Code
graph TD
    subgraph BERT Pipeline
        A[Raw Text] --> B[Tokenise]
        B --> C[Fine-tune Model]
        C --> D[Predict Label]
    end
    subgraph GenAI Pipeline
        E[Raw Text] --> F[Write Prompt]
        F --> G[Send to LLaMA API]
        G --> H[Parse Response]
    end





graph TD
    subgraph BERT Pipeline
        A[Raw Text] --> B[Tokenise]
        B --> C[Fine-tune Model]
        C --> D[Predict Label]
    end
    subgraph GenAI Pipeline
        E[Raw Text] --> F[Write Prompt]
        F --> G[Send to LLaMA API]
        G --> H[Parse Response]
    end






For evaluation, I used the F1-score, which balances precision and recall:
\[F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}\]

title: BERT vs GenAI for Complaint Detection
text:
Accuracy vs Speed
This is the trade-off that matters most in production. BERT is fast and accurate; GenAI is flexible but slow:


Code
fig = px.scatter(df, x="Latency_ms", y="Accuracy", color="Model", size="F1",
                 labels={"Latency_ms": "Latency (ms)", "Accuracy": "Accuracy"},
                 template="plotly_white")
fig.update_traces(marker=dict(size=20, line=dict(width=2, color='DarkSlateGrey')))
fig.show()


                                                
Accuracy vs inference speed for each model

title: BERT vs GenAI for Complaint Detection
text:
Where BERT Gets It Wrong
Even BERT makes mistakes. The confusion matrix shows it sometimes mislabels negative complaints as positive:


Code
z = [[150, 15], [20, 115]]
fig = px.imshow(z, text_auto=True,
                x=['Pred Negative', 'Pred Positive'],
                y=['Actual Negative', 'Actual Positive'],
                color_continuous_scale='Blues')
fig.show()


                                                
Confusion matrix for the fine-tuned BERT model

title: BERT vs GenAI for Complaint Detection
text:
What I Learned






Tip



Bottom line: If you have labelled data and need speed, go with BERT. If you need a quick prototype without training data, GenAI is surprisingly good — 88% accuracy with zero training.


In practice, I think the best approach is a hybrid: use GenAI to label your data, then fine-tune BERT on those labels. You get the best of both worlds.

Built with Plotly. Models from Hugging Face and Groq.

title: BERT vs GenAI for Complaint Detection
text:
Footnotes


Done as part of my MSc coursework at DCU.↩︎
